title: Deque ADT

publication.changeSummary: Language-neutral DSA draft with explicit C/C++/Java/Python mapping for Deque ADT; independent algorithm, implementation, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Algorithmic Foundations; August 2023; material applicable to Deque ADT
sources.claim: A deque supports insertion and removal at both front and back while preserving a sequence order.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A deque supports insertion and removal at both front and back while preserving a sequence order.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A sliding-window algorithm removes expired indexes from the front and dominated indexes from the back. Your goal is to use or implement a deque and justify it over a stack, queue or list. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Array representation and operations; Linked lists; Queue and circular buffer; Doubly and circular lists. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Shared concept and invariant
sections.body: A deque supports insertion and removal at both front and back while preserving a sequence order. This claim is assessed independently from syntax so conceptual evidence can transfer.

sections.heading: C, C++, Java, and Python implementation map
sections.body: In C, define an explicit array or linked representation and error contract; in C++, use vector/deque-backed adaptors with top/front semantics; in Java, program to Deque or Queue interfaces rather than legacy Stack; in Python, use list for stack and collections.deque for efficient queue/deque ends.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: It does not imply random middle access, and circular-buffer wraparound requires careful size invariants. The draft therefore rejects: A deque is just a queue with a faster middle insertion operation.

includes: Support insertion and removal at both ends and identify deque-suitable problems.
Use or implement a deque and justify it over a stack, queue or list.

In C, define an explicit array or linked representation and error contract; in C++, use vector/deque-backed adaptors with top/front semantics; in Java, program to Deque or Queue interfaces rather than legacy Stack; in Python, use list for stack and collections.deque for efficient queue/deque ends.
excludes: It does not imply random middle access, and circular-buffer wraparound requires careful size invariants.

outcomes: Use or implement a deque and justify it over a stack, queue or list.

examples.title: Deque ADT: language-neutral trace
examples.situation: A sliding-window algorithm removes expired indexes from the front and dominated indexes from the back.
walkthrough: Step 1 — State the abstract input and invariant: A sliding-window algorithm removes expired indexes from the front and dominated indexes from the back.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: A deque exposes both ends, not arbitrary middle insertion; maintain front, back, and size invariants.

walkthrough: Step 1 — Keep the same specification and invariant across all four languages.

Step 2 — Map storage, library, equality, bounds, ownership, and error rules using: In C, define an explicit array or linked representation and error contract; in C++, use vector/deque-backed adaptors with top/front semantics; in Java, program to Deque or Queue interfaces rather than legacy Stack; in Python, use list for stack and collections.deque for efficient queue/deque ends.
Step 3 — Retest this boundary in the selected implementation: It does not imply random middle access, and circular-buffer wraparound requires careful size invariants.
examples.result: Concept mastery transfers, while implementation evidence remains language-specific.

misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use an invariant or counterexample to explain why this DSA belief fails: A deque is just a queue with a faster middle insertion operation.
